title: Stopping chimney draughts: balloons, sheep and covers
description: An open chimney can waste around £60 a year in heat. Compare chimney balloons, wool draught excluders and caps, with costs, payback times and safety rules.
blurb: Chimney balloons, wool excluders and caps compared: what they cost, how fast they pay back, and how to fit one safely.
lede: An open fireplace is lovely for the six evenings a year you actually light it. For the other 8,754 hours it is a hole in your house, purpose-built to pull warm air out of the room and send it over the rooftops. The flue does not care whether a fire is burning: warm indoor air rises up it constantly, and cold outside air falls down it to replace what you have paid to heat. The fix costs about £20, takes two minutes to fit, and is one of the fastest-paying energy improvements available to anyone with an open chimney.
short_answer: Blocking an unused open chimney saves around £60 a year, according to the Energy Saving Trust. A chimney balloon or a wool draught excluder such as a Chimney Sheep costs roughly £20 to £35 and pays for itself in well under one heating season. Both are removable, so you can still light the fire whenever you want. Never block a flue that serves a working gas fire or boiler.

sections.h2: Why an open chimney costs you money all year round
sections.html:
A chimney works by the stack effect. Warm air is less dense than cold air, so it rises up the flue, and the taller and warmer the column, the harder it pulls. That is exactly what you want when a fire is lit, because it draws the smoke up and out. When the fire is not lit, the same physics keeps running: your centrally heated room air rises up the flue and is replaced by cold air pulled in under doors, through floorboards and around window frames.

The effect is two-way. On a cold, still day the flue mostly extracts your warm air. On a windy day, gusts across the chimney pot can push cold air down into the room as well, which is why an unused fireplace often feels like it is breathing cold air at your ankles. A draughty room also feels colder than a still room at the same temperature, and the usual response is to nudge the thermostat up.

The Energy Saving Trust puts the cost of an unblocked open chimney at around £60 a year for a typical home. At current gas prices that is roughly 800 kWh of heat going up the flue, which is more than many households spend running their fridge for the year. Chimney draughts sit alongside doors, floors and windows in the wider draught-proofing picture, but the chimney is usually the single biggest leak, and the cheapest one to plug.

sections.h2: The options compared: cost, saving and payback
sections.html:
There are four main ways to stop chimney draughts, plus a permanent option for fireplaces you will never use again. All of them deliver roughly the same saving, because they are all doing the same job of stopping airflow up the flue. The difference is price, convenience and whether you can still use the fire.

OptionTypical costAnnual savingPayback
Chimney balloon (inflatable)£20 to £25Around £604 to 5 months
Wool draught excluder (Chimney Sheep type)£20 to £35Around £604 to 7 months
Flue blocker or damper plate, DIY fitted£30 to £60Around £606 to 12 months
Chimney cap or cowl, professionally fitted£150 to £300Around £603 to 5 years

Saving based on the Energy Saving Trust estimate of around £60 a year for draught-proofing an unused open chimney, checked June 2026. At the gas unit rate of 7.33p per kWh (Ofgem cap, July to September 2026), £60 is roughly 800 kWh of wasted heat.

The payback figures only count the measured heat saving. The room also feels warmer and less draughty straight away, with less soot dust, fewer outside smells and quieter wind noise thrown in for the same £20.

sections.h2: Chimney balloons: cheap, effective, slightly fiddly
sections.html:
A chimney balloon is an inflatable bag made of tough laminated plastic. You push it up into the flue just above the fireplace opening, inflate it by mouth or with a small hand pump through a tube, and it expands to press against the flue walls and seal the gap. A small vent passage is built in so the flue still gets a trickle of air movement, which helps keep the chimney dry.

Fitting takes a few minutes:

  • Measure the flue where you intend to fit it, usually 30 cm or so above the opening where the flue narrows. Reach up with a tape measure and a torch. Most standard flues are around 23 cm by 23 cm (9 by 9 inches), but older and larger fireplaces vary a lot.
  • Buy the size that overlaps your measurements; the balloon needs to press firmly against all four sides.
  • Partially inflate, position it, then top up until it holds firm. Give it a gentle tug to check it will not drop.
  • Hang the supplied warning tag in the fireplace so nobody lights a fire under it.

The weaknesses are worth knowing. Balloons can slowly lose pressure over months, so check them each autumn as part of your pre-winter routine. They do not like flues with sharp protruding mortar or heavy tar deposits, which can puncture them. And if someone lights a fire underneath one, it is designed to deflate and drop out rather than block smoke, but you will be buying a new one. Treated gently, they last several years.

sections.h2: The Chimney Sheep: wool plugs that breathe
sections.html:
A wool chimney draught excluder, of which the Cumbrian-made Chimney Sheep is the best known, is a thick disc or rectangle of felted Herdwick wool on a plastic handle. You simply push it up into the flue until the wool compresses against the sides and grips. No inflating, no valves, nothing to puncture. The maker quotes a draught reduction of over 90%, and independent testing has supported figures in that region.

Wool has one genuine advantage over plastic here: it is breathable. Moisture vapour can pass slowly through the felt, so the flue above keeps a small amount of air exchange and is less prone to condensation and damp staining on chimney breasts. The wool plug deliberately does not seal 100%, and that is the correct design for a chimney, which should never be hermetically sealed. The same logic applies across the house: you want to stop draughts while keeping deliberate ventilation, a balance covered in more depth in our guide to ventilation without losing heat.

Sizes run from around 20 cm rounds up to large rectangles for inglenook-style openings, with custom sizes available. Measure the flue interior above the gather, not the fireplace opening, and buy slightly larger than your measurement so the wool compresses for a snug fit. Like the balloon, it pulls out in seconds when you want a fire, and the handle hangs down as a visible reminder that the flue is blocked. A wool excluder should last a decade or more, and at end of life it can go in the compost.

For renters, both balloons and wool excluders are ideal: no fixings, no alterations, and they move out with you.

If the fireplace is purely decorative and you are certain you will never light it, you can deal with the draught at the top of the chimney instead of the bottom.

  • A chimney cap or capping cowl fits over the pot and blocks most of the opening while leaving ventilation slots. It stops rain, birds and the worst of the draught. The part itself often costs £20 to £60, but unless the pot is reachable from a ladder you are paying a roofer, and access is most of the bill. Expect £150 to £300 fitted, more if scaffolding is needed.
  • A half-moon or vented cap keeps the flue usable while reducing rain entry and downdraughts. Good for chimneys you use occasionally, but it does little for the steady upward heat loss, so pair it with a balloon or wool plug at the bottom.
  • Permanent capping and blocking means sealing the pot with a vented cap and closing the fireplace opening with a board or brickwork that includes an air vent. The golden rule is ventilation top and bottom: a completely sealed flue traps moisture and will eventually show as damp patches on the chimney breast.

One option to avoid: the folk remedy of stuffing the flue with a bin bag full of newspaper or an old pillow. It works on day one, but there is no handle and no warning tag, and one day somebody who does not know it is there may light a fire under it. Spend the £20 on a purpose-made excluder.

sections.h2: Safety first: when you must not block a chimney
sections.html:
Everything above applies to an open fireplace flue that serves nothing else. There are situations where blocking is dangerous:

  • Never block a flue serving a gas fire, gas boiler, oil boiler or solid fuel stove. These appliances need the flue to remove combustion gases, and blocking it risks carbon monoxide entering the room. If a gas fire sits in the hearth, the flue is in use even if you rarely switch the fire on.
  • Wood-burning and multifuel stoves do not need a chimney excluder. The stove and its register plate already seal the flue, and the air controls on the stove manage the draught. If a room with a stove still feels draughty, the leak is somewhere else.
  • Fit a carbon monoxide alarm in any room with a working flue or appliance regardless. They cost from around £15 and are required by law in rented homes in most of the UK.
  • Always use the warning tag. Every reputable balloon and wool excluder ships with one. Hang it where anyone laying a fire will see it.

If in any doubt about what a flue serves, especially in an older house with multiple chimney pots, get a chimney sweep or Gas Safe engineer to confirm before blocking anything.

If you have an open fireplace you rarely use, a chimney draught excluder is about as close to free money as home energy efficiency gets: roughly £20 spent, around £60 a year saved, payback inside one winter, and the room feels warmer the same evening. Choose a wool excluder for fit-and-forget breathability, a balloon for awkward flue sizes, or a fitted cap if the fireplace is permanently decorative.

Then bank the saving and move down the list. Chimneys are the biggest single draught in most older homes, but doors, floors and letterboxes add up too, starting with draught-proofing the front door. And if the loft has less than 270 mm of insulation, that is the next job, because loft insulation saves several times what the chimney does. Together these small jobs cut the amount of heating you need to buy in the first place, which beats any tariff switch.

faq.q: Can I still use my fireplace with a chimney draught excluder fitted?
faq.a: Yes. Chimney balloons and wool excluders are designed to be removable. Pull the excluder out before lighting a fire, let the flue warm through, and refit it once the fire is fully out and the flue is cold. Always hang the supplied warning tag in the fireplace so nobody lights a fire while the flue is blocked.

faq.q: Is it safe to block a chimney that has a gas fire in it?
faq.a: No. Never block a flue that serves any working appliance, including gas fires, back boilers and stoves. The flue removes combustion gases, and blocking it creates a carbon monoxide risk. Chimney draught excluders are only for open fireplaces that serve nothing else. If you are unsure what a flue serves, ask a chimney sweep or Gas Safe engineer first.

faq.q: Will blocking my chimney cause damp?
faq.a: Not if some ventilation remains. Wool excluders are breathable by design, and chimney balloons include a small vent passage, so both keep enough air movement to stop condensation in the flue. Permanent capping is where damp problems happen: a sealed flue needs a vented cap at the top and an air vent at the fireplace opening to stay dry.

faq.q: What size chimney draught excluder do I need?
faq.a: Measure the inside of the flue, not the fireplace opening. Reach up past the gather with a tape measure and a torch, about 30 cm above the opening, and measure both dimensions. Standard UK flues are often around 23 by 23 cm, but older houses vary. Buy a wool excluder slightly larger than your measurement so it compresses to grip, or a balloon whose size range spans your measurement.

faq.q: How much does an open chimney actually cost in wasted heat?
faq.a: The Energy Saving Trust estimates around £60 a year for a typical home with an unused open chimney. At the July to September 2026 capped gas rate of 7.33p per kWh, that is roughly 800 kWh of heat lost up the flue each year. A £20 excluder therefore pays for itself in around four months of the heating season.
